title: Of Every Fruit Thou Mayest Freely Eat
subtitle: (Essay for PHIL0211 - Modern Political Philosophy)
content:
John Locke’s famous _Second Treatise of Government_ describes what is regarded as
the thinker’s most influential program: the emergence, limits, and value of
property rights. Locke’s thought is that property originates in what is now
known as “labor mixing”—one is said to “own” an object upon applying labor to it
such that the object yields some value. This value, Locke says, can be
distributed to others such that the object is of net benefit to the community at
large. Since Locke’s argument is buttressed on this ostensible benefit to
individuals other than the property owner, Locke finds it proper to qualify his
stance by appending to his description the so called Lockean proviso: that “no
man but he can have a right to what that is once joined to, _at least where there is enough, and as good, left in common for others_” (19). 
There is strength
to these ideas, and history to show for it: the founding tenets of the current
chief mode of economy are direct applications of Locke’s thinking. Despite this,
the discussion that follows will outline the theory’s shortcomings and present
them as results of Locke’s ill-defined property rights; specifically, I contend
that because property is a superstructure of the state of nature (as can be
shown by Locke’s own account), the program of property ownership that the Second
Treatise outlines is contradictory and self-effacing. I will do this in three
stages: first, I argue that property rights are nether parallel to nor more
fundamental than, but rather _emergent from_ the state of nature; then I will turn
to showing that the limitations of property rights—as Locke himself lays them
out—naturally lead to a degree of “unreasonable” use of property, for property
use needs not be as reasonable as anything else; finally, I will conclude by
arguing that these unreasonable uses of property are, by Locke’s state of
nature, just cause to disarm the property owner of their property, thereby
placing a natural limitation on property rights unaccounted for by the thinker
himself.

The state of nature, by Locke’s design, is more fundamental than all
other constructs of a political philosophy—thus these remaining constructs
should be taken as dependent on the state of nature and, most importantly, a
state of governance does not imply the constituency cannot be in a state of
nature. This is of note because it grants one the liberty of discussing features
of the state of nature in spite of possibly contrary features of sovereign
legislation; in particular, it permits us to discuss elements of the state of
nature that help deconstruct Locke’s theory of property. The state of nature’s
feature of interest is the tendency to “preserve [oneself]” as well as “the rest
of mankind,” which necessitates the ability to “punish the transgressors of that
law”—a right which we will see becomes instrumental in delimiting property
rights. Yet Locke describes the inception of civil society as a renunciation of
the right to “private judgement” in favor of a communal “umpire,” requiring an
abandonment of this crucial natural right to punish “transgressors” (9,
46). I am nevertheless compelled to challenge this interface between the states
of nature and civility, for the so-called _right to revolution_ is introduced by
Locke in order to ensure the sovereignty does not legislate contrary to the
preservation of humanity it was formed under the
pretense of upholding. Locke argues that the particular right the violation of
which warrants revolution is indeed the right to property—he writes that
“whenever the legislators endeavor to take away, and destroy the property of the
people… they put themselves into a state of war with the people”—but I contend
that the right to property is to be upheld precisely because it poses a supposed
benefit to humanity, and that other violations of the preservation of humanity
should likewise be met with a state of war. For Locke sees fit to add the
“[reduction] of [the populace] to slavery under arbitrary power” as a condition
that may invoke the right to revolution (111). Such “slavery” is of course
to be repudiated on the grounds that it infringes on natural rights, freedoms,
and preservations of humanity, so to speak, which are arguably punishable by the
state of nature.

Sovereignty is then not a substitution of the state of nature, rather a state
that extends from, depends on, and is defined in relation to the state of
nature. This is an interpretation supported not only by the conditions of the
right to revolution but also by Locke’s own definitions: “to understand
political power right,” he writes, “and derive it from its original, we must
consider what state men are naturally in, and that is, a state of perfect
freedom to order their action” (8). Here Locke motivates his study of the
state of nature by defining it as constituting the “original” from which
“political power” arises—as opposed to defining it in relation to sovereignty or
political power—and thus one construes the remaining elements of Locke's theory
as emerging from conditions _additional_ to the those of the
state of nature. Among these elements is indeed his theory of property, although
this does not initially appear to be the case in his treatment of it. After all,
Locke claims the “natural” right to property is justified by the divine
provision of abundant earthly resources: “God, who hath given the world to men
in common, hath also given them reason to make use of it to the best advantage
of life, and convenience. The earth, and all that is therein, is given to men
for the support and comfort of their being” (18). This is, however, the
only deontological claim Locke makes in discussing property—the remainder of the
program lacks in a description of, say, how individuals in a state of nature
ought to _interact_ with property rights. In fact, the preservation of property
ownership is entirely hinged on the claimed benefit that individual ownership
poses to the general populace, for under the state of nature individuals reserve
the right to reprimand and punish violations of the sanctity of life. From this
one infers that property rights are only unalienable insofar as “the property of
labor should be able to over-balance the community of land”; in other words,
that for the right to property to be absolute, these proclaimed benefits must
universally hold for any piece of property under any circumstance (25).
This is all to say that, despite God’s having bestowed upon humanity a plethora
of available natural resources (i.e., despite the ostensibly divine right one
has to _obtain_ property), the right to _continue owning_ that property is
contingent on that property being of greater benefit than harm to the larger
populace, and thus what one construes as the right to property—but which really
is something like “the right to continue claiming an object as property”—is in
fact not at all fundamental nor unalienable.

Granted, Locke’s claim is
precisely that an individual’s labor over natural resources will _consistently_
produce yields useful to all, thereby rendering the right to property
consistently immune to objection—modulo the Lockean proviso. Taking this claim
at face value leads to a reading of Locke that seemingly permits boundless
accumulation of property under the guise of a concomitant benefit to others. The
above discussion on the state of nature’s precedency over property rights
challenges this justification for limitless property accumulation, but it
remains to show that Locke’s claim of property ownership’s positive moral
value is ill-conceived. I propose that owning and maintaining property within
the bounds of the Lockean proviso is an act _devoid_ of moral value, and that
ownership of sufficient property to violate the preservation of humanity is not
only morally
improper but a natural consequence of Locke’s theory of property.
The proviso,
of course, places a natural limit on the amount of natural resources one can
appropriate for oneself—with the earth’s fruits distributed to all individuals
for their survival and enjoyment, and with the use of these fruits being a
divine right, it follows that exploiting this right until
others’ abilities to exercise it are compromised constitutes a violation of
natural rights. In fact, one may claim that the state of nature is precisely
what permits punishing against transgressions of the proviso: if human beings
are at birth entitled to sufficient resources for self-preservation, it follows
that appropriating the available resources so thoroughly that the remaining
resources are insufficient for others to exercise said self-preservation poses
an offence to the preservation of humanity. Thus, the state of nature’s clause
on punishment equips with the right to enforce the proviso, which is to show
that my supposition that the state of nature precedes right to property is
indeed consistent with the limitations Locke himself sets. 

Now, the proviso is nevertheless underdefined—after all, perhaps we should
demand Locke clarify the _types of resources_ that should be available to others
(for as it stands, the proviso allows, say, a hegemony on one particular good,
provided other goods remain in sufficient abundance), or the _amount_ of leftover
resources that is deemed sufficient for others. Yet regardless of any additional
interpretation one may seek to draw from Locke’s writings in order to fill these
gaps, there is the deeper limitation placed on property rights by the
aforementioned universality of the state of nature, and the moral valuelessness
of property ownership. Indeed, labor mixing need not yield goods that are of
benefit to humanity—nor goods that are harmful, for that matter. Disregarding
any deontological morality—such as Locke’s—that might be attached to labor, one
may easily argue that the work over natural resources is an action like any
other, and thus should be studied with the same impartiality as all other
actions. For example, one could labor over fertile land and choose to either
hoard or discard the fruits, thereby stripping other individuals of the
potential to obtain those fruits; or, in a more extreme example, one could labor
over ores to carve weapons with which they may kill. If we are to permit such
misuses or abuses of resources, we must likewise permit the populace to exercise
their natural right to “punishment of transgressors” and, in a manner of
speaking, “infringe” on the right to property of the perpetrator. This is, 
granted, given proper conditions to exercise features of the state of
nature—i.e., given the civil state does not legislate against such matters. Of
course, the civil state should legislate against abuse of natural resources, for
Locke’s theory necessitates a government’s investment in the sanctity of
humanity; then, ordinarily, there little room for the general populace to
deliberate on what is and is not deemed a transgression and what is and is not a
proper punishment. Yet, as has been argued above, individuals are entitled to
their natural judgement if a sovereignty is found to be either unrightfully
seizing property or enslaving the populace. With that, I suggest that the state
need not be exploiting its people out of its own volition—_neglect_ of extraneous
exploitative forces is sufficient to implicate the sovereign and place the
citizenry in a state of war against the state; namely, if resources are being
exploited or misused, or if the proviso is blatantly violated, and legislation
does not address these matters—or perhaps is in support of them—the
populace reserves the right to invoke a state of war, reintroduce features of
the state of nature, and pressure the sovereignty to either revise its legislation or
entirely dissolve. With civil government and property rights being constructs
superposed over the state of nature, I further argue that the degree of natural
judgement that the constituency will exercise—as well as the particular facets
of civil life that will serve as proxies for the state of war—is not binary nor
discrete, but rather continuous and multifarious. That is, the conditions that
are sufficient for the right of revolution may be as immediate as physical harm
through property or as abstract as permanent and/or global damage to the
environment, and the “revolution” itself may be as tame as a civil discourse or
as brash as an insurrection; all of this, of course, because the state of nature
is a “state of perfect freedom,” a state which permits individuals choice of
punishment. 

I recognize it is important to acknowledge the influence Locke’s
thoughts on property bear on present-day life, and that I must thereby
acknowledge the possibly difficult implications the above discourse may have on
the conducts of western civilization. Perhaps I have inadvertently justified a
unanimous declaration of war against all modern political infrastructure, or
perhaps I have invalidated Locke’s theory of civil society to the point that all
further discussion is fruitless under my interpretation of the thinker. Yet I
remain steadfast to the driving mechanisms of the argument: that the state of
nature is indeed universal and only on occasion compromised, that labor mixing
holds no special moral value, and that these two ideas work in conjunction to
ensure a natural control of property accumulation. This all may place
citizens in a constant and cyclical revolution against their own sovereign,
but if that is the case, so be it.

### Work Cited 
Locke, John. [_Second Treatise of Civil Government_](https://www.hackettpublishing.com/philosophy/second-treatise-of-government). Hackett, 1980, Indianapolis.

title: Sense Without Reference
subtitle: (Short Essay for PHIL1860 - Sense and Reference)
content:
> This is the principle of the theory of denoting I wish to
advocate: that denoting phrases never have any meaning in themselves,
but that every proposition in whose verbal expression they occur has a
meaning. (Bertrand Russell, "On Denoting", p. 480)

This passage sees Bertrand Russell crystallizing the theory of meaning
and denoting "On Denoting" puts forward and placing it in opposition to
the theories of meaning of his contemporaries. In particular, as we will
see, he intends to underscore the ultimate effect his theory of denoting
has on the notion of meaning as it appears in denoting phrases; these
characterizing features, he later argues, are precisely what makes his
theory more desirable (in particular, in my reading, *better defined*)
than, say, Gottlob Frege's. The reasoning that yields this result from
Russell's principles of denotation is as follows.

Russell suggests that a denotation is nothing more than a propositional
function, which necessarily means---and this is key---it must be
supplied a variable to attain a meaning. When we make statements
containing the word *the*, there is an implicit quantifier and an
implicit assertion of uniqueness: there exists some thing, and that is
*the* thing, and that thing also satisfies whatever the remainder of the
proposition contains. The denoting phrase constitutes the "that is *the*
thing" portion of our proposition, and can be written formally as, say,
$Fx$; notably, without the quantifier, the propositional function has no
truth value, which, to Russell, signifies meaninglessness. Here one
could investigate whether Russell is equating definite truth value with
meaning, or that at the very least truth value is a necessary but
possibly insufficient condition for meaning, but this matter would take
us too far astray. Particularities aside, this is precisely what Russell
means by "the denoting phrases never have any meaning in themselves." It
is by fitting the denoting phrase in a proposition that we extract a
meaning from denotation: the proposition introduces a quantifier which
supplies an argument which evaluates the truth value of the denoting
phrase. "Verbal expression" then really amounts to "the semantics of the
sentence which represents that underlying proposition," and we come to
the understanding that utterances must stand for propositions about the
denotation in order to set off the aforementioned mechanism which endows
it with meaning.

Through this construction we do away with the Fregean concept of
"sense"; indeed, this is among the aims of Russell's paper. In
particular, we can argue that Russell's view does not support denoting
phrases with different senses but identical references: there is no way
a denoting phrase can have different different senses in isolation if
the denoting phrase has no meaning, and two (otherwise identical)
propositions containing different denoting phrases referencing the same
object must evaluate to the same truth value, thereby bearing the same
meaning. There are a number of reasons one might wish to provide an
improvement or alternative to Frege's theory---for instance, the
under-definition of sense, which is obviously circumvented by
Russel---but Russell fixates chiefly on the issue of "sense without
reference." The claim is that we can make obviously truth-valued
statements about nonexistent objects, thereby constructing an ostensibly
nonsensical, reference-less sentence with a paradoxically definite
meaning; while Russell exemplifies this pathology with "the King of
France is bald"---which is, he says, "plainly false"---this seems to me
a, for the lack of a better term, "vacuously false" statement, and I
actually believe the phenomenon is better illustrated by an example not
dissimilar to Frege's own "least rapidly converging series" case
(Gottlob Frege, "On Sense and Reference", p. 58). E.g., "the largest
even number is not prime" is a true statement about something that does
not exist, modulo concerns about its *a priori* nature. Compare this
with the baldness of the hypothetical French king—"the King of France
is not bald" is as true as "the King of France is bald," but "the
largest even number is prime" is sure to raise eyebrows. We see a clear
difference in truth value between a certain description of the
nonexistent largest even number and the negation of that same
description, suggesting the sentence is indeed endowed with meaning.

Under Russell's theory this is no longer an issue—Russell's version of
this denoting phrase *contains* the proposition that there *is* a
largest even number (and there is only one largest even number, and it
is not prime, so on, but existence is the pivotal operand Russell
introduces). Thus it is the case that any proposition about the largest
even number is false, that sentences about the largest even number
recover their meaning, and that this reasoning generalizes to all
sentences without reference. In fact, Russell's claim is stronger than
it seems: he says that *every* proposition containing a denoting phrase
has a meaning, i.e., we *cannot* construct a meaningless proposition
containing a denoting phrase. Note that—and I believe this is an
important subtlety to elucidate—Russell says every *proposition*
containing a denoting phrase is meaningful, not that every *sentence*
containing a denoting phrase is meaningful. One can imagine he choose
this phrasing to handle the case of a sentence nonsensical due to a part
other than a denoting phrase, under the assumption that propositions
cannot be nonsensical. That is to say, something like: the type of
sentence Russell is considering necessarily expresses a proposition that
would have a definite truth value if we were to, say, switch out the
reference of the denoting phrase for another reference. Edge case such
as the famous "this sentence is false" are easily dismissed as they are
not propositions, as are imperatives such as "find the largest even
number" (although the fact that one can immediately respond to the
latter with "that's impossible!" does bring into question the possible
meaningfulness of non-propositions about nonexistent objects; I sidestep
this matter as I am not familiar with the analysis of non-propositions).

The question of nonexistent references addressed, Russell's theory may
be further applied to additional puzzles and provide a few other
desiderata—or so he argues. One is the issue of identity: tautologies
such as "$x$ is $x$" carry no knowledge, yet provided two different
denotations of the same thing, one can use everyday language to state
$x=x$ in a manner that *is* seemingly charged with knowledge; see
Russell's "Scott was the author of *Waverley*" example in p. 492. This
is one of Frege's primary concerns, and it is arguably the entire
motivation for his introduction of "sense." Russell, seeking to show
that his formulation can supplant Frege's, explains that his deferring
of meaning to a proposition bearing a denotation modifies the semantics
of such an identification such that the proposition is not simply "$x$
is $x$" but instead something richer: by identifying two denoting
phrases we make the proposition that when a variable satisfies one of
those denotations' propositional functions, the variable is identical to
any variable that satisfies the other denotation's propositional
function. This, of course, is not a tautology.

As a closing remark, I mention that Russell's theory of denoting also
serves him by supplementing his classification of knowledge ("Knowledge
by Acquaintance and Knowledge by Description"). He infers that his
theory carries consequences for descriptive knowledge: statements about
things with which one is not acquainted are reducible to propositions
the constituents of which are things with which one *is* acquainted. The
mind example—"so-and-so has a mind which has such and such
properties," where we are not acquainted with so-and-so's mind
itself—is taken to mean something like: for all $x$, if $x$ is
so-and-so's mind, $x$ it has such and such properties (p. 493). We are
presumably acquainted with so-and-so's mind and acquainted with the set
of properties, and no explicit mention is made of the mind itself; we
leave that as an argument which is supplied by the quantifier. Crudely
speaking, we need not know the mind—perhaps not even whether the mind
in question exists—but only that if something satisfies the property
of being that mind, it satisfies a number of other properties. Here it
may be fruitful to question how far we need reduce denotations of
objects to be satisfied (by, say, investigating what it means to be
acquainted with a person and breaking this apart into more granular
acquainted objects and repeating the process), but this seems to me a
question of epistemology and lies outside the scope of the preceding
discussion.
summary:
> This is the principle of the theory of denoting I wish to
advocate: that denoting phrases never have any meaning in themselves,
but that every proposition in whose verbal expression they occur has a
meaning. (Bertrand Russell, "On Denoting", p. 480)

This passage sees Bertrand Russell crystallizing the theory of meaning
and denoting "On Denoting" puts forward and placing it in opposition to
the theories of meaning of his contemporaries. In particular, as we will

has on the notion of meaning as it appears in denoting phrases; these
characterizing features, he later argues, are precisely what makes his
theory more desirable (in particular, in my reading, *better defin

title: Dance as Weapons
subtitle: Civil Simulacra and the Human Material (for PHIL0211)
content:
In the pantheon of history’s greatest rebuttals, few can claim as much fame as
Karl Marx’s treatment of fellow Young Hegelian Bruno Bauer’s writings on the
emancipation of the European Jews in the 19th century—at the time an ongoing
process which evidently perplexed the politics of thinkers such as the Young
Hegelians. His response—the unfortunately titled _On the Jewish Question_—used the
eponymous political issue to launch into a dissection of liberty or emancipation
as an absolute as opposed to emancipation as it relates to a governing body, as
well as the process of abstracting the desire for absolute, material
emancipation into an immaterial, state-dependent desire. These ideas resemble a
specter of later writings of Marx’s—writings which describe the alienation of
labor as a similar dialectical process and propose the infamous economic system
of Communism as an inevitable conclusion to these processes—and such as are
heralded as essential components to Marx’s thought. Indeed, Bauer’s discussion
of Jewish emancipation as relative to the state and Christian emancipation
exemplifies quite well the antithesis of Marx’s thought—that such structures are
independent of material, physical, spatial conditions, and that one is thus
perfectly capable of handling notions of “states” without treatment of more
primitive substances. _On the Jewish Question_ then becomes a resounding negation
of Bauer’s stance, an assertion that, universally, human liberty is a different
and more profound type of liberty than civil liberty. With that, much like how
Marx engages with Bauer to produce these claims, I will engage with Marx to
further develop his theory of liberty and propose that the advocacy for civil
liberty must in fact be annihilated in order for the truer human liberty to be
actualized, for the existence of a civil liberty in fact perpetuates existing
civil states that oppose human emancipation. 

Put briefly, Bauer’s thesis is that the Jewish cause is but one facet of a
larger cause which Jews and Christians alike have stake in: the eradication of
all forms of religious discrimination through the secularization of the state.
Marx characterizes this argument as akin to the sentiment “we have to emancipate
ourselves before we can emancipate others” (28). This, he believes, is to
support Bauer’s opinion that the resolution of the “Jewish question”
necessitates Jewish citizens “cease to be Jewish,” as a completely secular state
must necessarily disallow the practice of religion: “the political abolition of
religion,” Marx says, “is the abolition of all religion” (29). In other words,
Marx is under the impression that, to Bauer, the eradication of strata within a
given facet of civil life requires the eradication of that facet itself, perhaps
because that facet is naturally self-stratifying or because stratification is an
immutable human tendency. Then it follows that some force must actively oppose
the free practice of religion to ensure the liberty from stratification that
Bruno characterizes the Jewish constituency to be demanding—and similarly for
other emancipation movements. The scope of this opposition, of course, demands
that the force be legislative, hence the unavoidable inclusion of the state in
Bauer’s reply to the Jewish question. 

This is where Marx begins to take issue with Bauer. In proposing that the
solution to Jewish emancipation is founded on legislation (or, one could argue,
in proposing the solution should at all be constructed from the state), Bauer
has neglected a different, wholly novel flavor of liberation, much to the
detriment of his “solution.” According to Marx, the colloquial notion of
emancipation is underdefined, and there in fact exists “_political_ emancipation”
and “_human_ emancipation” the “relation between” which he claims is unexamined by
Bauer (30). The emancipation which Bauer treats is the former: emancipation
manifested as legislation, emancipation overseen and carried out by the state,
often an _active_ opposition to the construct from which the populace is
emancipated. Marx would like the _latter_ form of emancipation to be discussed: a
form Marx defines as the negation of political emancipation, a form that is
non-relative, independent of the state, and suggested to be the most desirable.
Marx writes that “to be _politically_ emancipated from religion is not to be
finally and completely emancipated from religion, because political emancipation
is not the final and absolute form of human emancipation.” Instead, political
emancipation constitutes the proclamation of sovereign agnosticism toward social
categories: “[the state] proclaims… that every member of society is an _equal_
partner in popular sovereignty” (32-33). This is to say that political liberty
is a liberty written in the vocabulary of the state and contained only in the
text of the state: it is a symbolic liberty, embalmed and immutable, existing in
the simulacrum of legislation as opposed to in physical space.  Human
emancipation, meanwhile, is the physical liberty that political emancipation
simulates, marked by the destruction of physical stratification—spatial
separation, material possessions, objects-of-labor. Indeed, Marx’s
prioritization of human emancipation conforms to the materialist theory he would
later develop: the categorization of social functions such as religion may be
nominally legislative—or even ideological or cultural—but its origin and
substance is material, spatial, and physical, not intellectual. The ideological
simulacrum of these social stratifications can then—to borrow Marx’s
terminology—be considered a _superstructure_ of material arrangements, emerging
from the material in a form that reinforces and perpetuates physical conditions.

At the interface of the superstructure of politics and the base material
substance lies the economy—in particular, the status of property ownership in a
particular state guides the formation of the policy, ideology, and culture which
we have argued work to preserve the base. Indeed, Marx claims that despite labor
underpinning political economy, “to labor [political economy] gives nothing, and
to private property everything.” The underlying mechanism Marx attributes this
paradox to is his famous “labor alienation” process, of which he claims private
property is a “necessary consequence” (79). He further contends that the
alienation of labor is at the source of every condition from which humanity
could seek liberty—“the emancipation of the workers contains universal human
emancipation,” he writes, “because the whole of human servitude is involved in
the relation of the worker to production”—and as such one can characterize the
purer “human” liberty as a process targeting important _physical_ modes of
exploitation such as labor alienation (79-80). In fact, one sees that the
aforementioned political simulacra in fact _emerge from_ labor alienation, for it
is precisely the process which replaces an object with its specter—material
objects with, to use Baudrillardian terms, hyperreal representations of the
production: say, wage. With humans themselves existing in physicality and not in
any hyperreality, and with the entirety of individuals’ physical consequences on
the world thereby substituted with virtual objects, this becomes a process of,
in Marx’s words, “estranging from man nature _and himself_.” This estranged
self-image is precisely the “citizen” in Marx’s “citizen”-“living individual”
dialectic: the subject of political emancipation, which lives in the text of the
sovereign and is spawned by labor alienation (75, 34).

This is all to strengthen the power of Marx’s communist economic design: the
model seeks to unseat the existing superstructural policies and cultures—a
“_positive_ expression of annulled private property,” he says—in order to
redistribute the product of labor to workers and, given Marx’s labor-centric
characterization of oppression, purportedly secure universal human emancipation.
Marx intends communism as “the _positive_ transcendence of _private property_, or
_human self-estrangement_,” and “the true resolution of the strife between
existence and essence”—suggesting that indeed this program would eventually
reunify the “citizen” with the “living individual,” producing conditions
necessary for the supposed absolute and final human emancipation to be realized
(84). This is all framed, of course, as a process that orbits labor-laborer
unification—the additional features of this utopia are simply corollaries. Yet
Marx’s formulation of the political and human lives suggests an opposing force
to the realization of communism as he wills it: I suggest that if one is to
consider the citizen-living individual dichotomy with gravity, they must admit
that the continued existence of this virtual, estranged self in fact not only
complicates the strategy of emancipation, but also effaces the real self which
is the subject of _human_ emancipation. Marx’s proposed transition _into_ a
communist economy ostensibly takes this obstacle into account: “the Communists,”
the _Communist Manifesto_ reads, “openly declare their ends can be attained only
by the _forcible_ overthrow of all existing social conditions.” For this effort to
be effective, there must be class-wide cooperation so that the workers’ produce,
essential to the livelihood of the bourgeoisie, is effectively held for ransom;
according to Marx, increasingly decadent working conditions and shrinking,
densely-populated working environments such as factories will catalyze this
ideological unity. Yet I argue that Marx has overseen strong sources of divide
within the proletariat, namely the effacing property of the political
simulacrum: the working-class constituency will disagree not only on methods to
use to achieve their cause, but also on what the form of that cause is (in
particular, the form of the oppressor) as well as, in more extreme cases,
whether that cause exists at all. They unequivocally share a true, material,
physical oppressor—and this is the actual object of their struggle—but the
estrangement of the proletariat’s “citizen self” from the material self erases
the marks of this physical oppressor and replaces them with simulated, virtual
oppressors. Emancipatory discourse among the proletariat then becomes a skirmish
of simulated, performative ideologies taking place in a non-physical and
abstract arena. Indeed, this might be a literally _virtual_ setting—I would be
amiss to not mention the degree to which 21st century communication technology
has exacerbated this situation, creating self-reinforcing ideological
information loops, catalyzing radical thought, rendering irony and gravity
indistinguishable—yet this is only the most tangible iteration of a process of
political abstraction that has been long in development. 

Physical proximity is insufficient to engender a coherent proletarian praxis
capable of material action. The further requirement is the annihilation of the
immaterial political personas that the proletariat has been conditioned to fight
for emancipation through: the “citizens.” This may occur naturally—perhaps a
cataclysmic breaking point will be reached in the increasingly splintering and
detached virtual politics, or perhaps environmental deterioration will force the
working masses into a violent confrontation with physicality—or it may require
deliberate instigation from within the political simulacrum. Either way, the
process cannot begin without a break out of illusory emancipatory gestures, for
otherwise the proletariat will continue to live in their “citizen” projection,
not only seeking immaterial victories but also believing that immaterial
gestures are material ones, that superstructural politico-economic complexes are
the essence of arrangements of matter and not the converse. 

This is not to discredit the Marx’s thought: the high spatial density of
laborers was indeed a novel feature of industrial society at the time of Marx’s
conception. However, not only has history shown this was a condition
insufficient for catalysis, but this is a condition that is gradually
dissipating, giving way to low-density intellectual workspaces, work-from-home
models, and “millennialized” idyllic workspaces (see the campuses of the
technology oligopoly). Over this, the youth’s political selves—for which the
internet has become a proxy—have mutated beyond recognition, yielding a thousand
hyper-niche ideologies each irreconcilably incompatible with other another,
further obfuscating the shared material struggle underpinning their ideological
angst. Together, these conditions suggest that the only effective approach to
human emancipation is one which entirely _rejects_ political emancipation, one
which embraces physicality, embraces the material of the human—which we have
seen, capital is willing to put at stake!—an approach which releases the
bustling potential energy of immaterial and virtual political discourse into a
concentrated kinetic strike.

### Work Cited 
Marx, Karl, and Friedrich Engels. [_The Marx-Engels
Reader_](https://wwnorton.com/books/9780393090406). Edited by Robert Tucker, 2nd
Revised & enlarged, W. W. Norton & Company, 1978.
summary:
In the pantheon of history’s greatest rebuttals, few can claim as much fame as
Karl Marx’s treatment of fellow Young Hegelian Bruno Bauer’s writings on the
emancipation of the European Jews in the 19th century—at the time an ongoing
process which evidently perplexed the politics of thinkers such as the Young
Hegelians. His response—the unfortunately titled _On the Jewish Question_—used the
eponymous political issue to launch into a dissection of liberty or emancipation
as an absolute as opposed to emancipation as it relates to a governing body, as
well as the process of abstracting the desire for absolute, material
emancipation into an immaterial, state-dependent desire. These ideas resemble a
specter of later writings of Marx’s—writings w

title: Ignorance, Error, and Description
subtitle: On Kripke's Arguments Against Description Theory
content:
Saul Kripke, positioned against the description theory of reference (or,
as it is also known, the cluster theory) spends much of his second
lecture in the *Naming and Necessity* series targeting the following two
core theses of the description theory: (2) "one of the properties \[in
the cluster a speaker believes to correspond to an expression\], or some
conjointly, are believed by \[the speaker\] to pick out some individual
uniquely," and (3) "if most, or a weighted most, of the \[properties\]
are satisfied by one unique object $\gamma$, then $\gamma$ is the referent of
\[the expression\]" (Saul Kripke, p. 71).^[Page references not otherwise marked
are to Kripke's *Naming and Necessity*.] He does so by means of two arguments,
reserved for theses (2) and (3) respectively,
for which I will borrow the terminology "argument from ignorance" and
"argument from error."^[Due to Richard Kimberly Heck.] In what follows, I will argue that these are
indeed distinct arguments, and that both are needed to make a strong
case against the description theory, but that the argument from
ignorance is more susceptible to counterattack by description theorists
than the argument from error.

Kripke begins with the argument from ignorance against thesis (2). The
argument from inference challenges the view that expressions that refer
are associated with descriptions precise enough to *uniquely* pick out
an object, emphasizing the uniqueness requirement. Kripke contends that
there are expressions which *do refer* but whose reference is fixed
(under the description theory) by descriptions too ambiguous to select
an object uniquely; in other words, he looks for an expression that
fails to refer according to thesis (2), but which does refer according
to intuition or common sense or in another demonstrable sense. If he is
successful, the description theory makes an incorrect prediction. To
this end, he selects two examples which he explores in parallel: that of
Cicero and that of Richard Feynman. He says that it is reasonable to
think that those names stand for descriptions of the sort "the
individual who did such-and-such," where such-and-such constitutes some
eminent, representative or otherwise characteristic achievement or
accolade of that person. This model is not hard to justify: it is not
hard to imagine an individual who is not well versed enough in ancient
history to name a property of Cicero other than that he was an ancient
Roman, or that he purportedly wrote *Lorem ipsum*, so on, although it
requires ignorance of, say, what Cicero looked like, or other sense-data
which can serve as unique properties (this point will bring us to a
discussion of acquaintance later). Namely, Kripke says: "Cicero" is
fixed by "the individual who was 'a famous Roman orator,' " "Richard
Feynman" is fixed by "the individual who is 'a physicist or something' "
(*ibid.*, p. 81). A third example, one which appeals less to fame, may
be "John is the individual who does maintenance at my apartment
complex."

These are examples of expressions fixed by descriptions too vague to
uniquely select a reference: there are many famous Roman orators, my
building is maintained by a whole cast of different individuals, and the
description "a physicist *or something*," I hope, speaks for itself;
presumably the speaker is *ignorant* of further, uniquely identifying
descriptions, hence the terminology. But these are also referents which,
according to common sense (or, Kripke's common sense, and the author's
in the John case) *do refer*, even in the case of an ignorant speaker.
There are names, Kripke admits, that are *constructed* by the
description theory (Jack the Ripper is whoever committed those murders,
St. Anne is whoever birthed the virgin Mary) and thereby skirt this
criticism, but Feynman, Cicero, and John are not such names, for it is
nonsensical to append "Richard Feynman, *whoever that may be*." Of
course, if you are a description theorist, you might say: actually,
utterances coming from ignorant speakers *do not refer*, even if the
speaker believes the contrary.

Kripke then turns to the argument from error against thesis (3). Now he
attacks the notion that, taking uniqueness for granted, the object
satisfying the description associated with an expression is indeed the
reference of that expression. This is where he conjures up the famous
Gödel-Schmidt example, briefly reproduced here for thoroughness: The
name "Gödel" is fixed as "the individual who discovered the
incompleteness theorems," but it turns out Kurt Gödel was not the
discoverer of said theorems (perhaps just the publisher) and some other
man Schmidt actually made the discovery. By the description theory,
"Gödel" in fact refers to Schmidt (in the sense of a semantic reference)
but, to Kripke, common sense/intuition has it that "Gödel" still refers
to Kurt Gödel.^[Although in some circumstances, it might be used in a "St. Anne"
fashion; such cases employ speaker's reference.] Kripke's reasons for believing
this are admittedly a bit mysterious, but they are elucidated by critique of his
claim. One reply is: the only circumstance in which someone fixes the name
"Gödel" according to the description Kripke gives (i.e., the association with
the theorems) is one where the speaker is not aware of sufficient facts
associated with the name "Gödel" to use the name correctly in the first
place. Kripke might say in defense: look, even when such an ignorant
speaker uses "Gödel," those who are privy to more specific knowledge
about Kurt Gödel---perhaps just enough knowledge to distinguish Gödel as
an individual possessing qualities other than having purportedly
discovered the incompleteness theorems---still associate the name
"Gödel" with the correct man, even if it is uttered by an ignorant
speaker. If the description theory is correct (more specifically,
applies to anything more than an idiolect) the non-ignorant listener
would in fact picture *Schmidt*.

For the sake of further illustration, let's take a rather extreme
example: as a child, I once had a dream that, following a nondescript
medical procedure, my father was replaced by an identical clone. Perhaps
he differed only in the number of hairs in his head, or some other
nearly imperceptible regard. I would like to think that I know my own
father well enough to refer to him by his name, "Harley," without having
to worry about possessing enough knowledge about him to form a set of
descriptions that might be erroneous in the Gödel-Schmidt sense. In this
scenario, when I use the name "Harley," I do not refer to the the man
living in my house pretending to be my father, I refer to my actual
father, who is elsewhere. If I was a description theorist, I would be
unable to express concern over the disappearance of my father in the
first place: this clone, by construction, fits every description that I
could list off (i.e., that consists of facts about my father I can
perceive), and so it would be absurd for me to approach my mother saying
"That's not the real Harley!" as I would only be saying "Harley is not
Harley!" I admit the extremity of this example might spell its
downfall---how can I possibly identify the clone as an impostor when he
is indeed a *clone*? Perhaps I can say that the same supernatural force
that replaced Harley in the first place also endowed me with the
knowledge that the replacement was made. Does that constitute a
"description" of the object which is the reference of "Harley"? I'm not
sure this is immediately the case. In the event that it isn't, we've
just shown that, even in possession of a maximal set of feasibly
attainable descriptions, description theory fails to make the correct
prediction.

I point out that these attacks on description theory are distinct;
neither reduces to the other. For one, as I earlier noted, attempts to
amend the description theory in the face of the argument from error
might appeal to an ignorant speaker in a fashion similar to the ignorant
speaker defense deployed against the argument from error. These are,
indeed, ignorance of the same kind: in both arguments Kripke gives, the
speaker does not know enough about the actual thing that bears that name
(to other speakers) to identify that thing with the name. I believe the
difference lies in the manner in which the description theory breaks
down in the presence of different types of missing descriptions. You may
have a strong enough description to pick out an object uniquely but
erroneously (by missing the appropriate descriptions), thereby
satisfying all the requirements the description theory lists for
referring to something yet still failing to do so in a manner that
matches our understanding of reference in daily usage. Or, you might
have a weak enough description to not pick out any object at all, while
the usage of the name will indeed be interpreted by others to pick out a
particular thing.

Description theorists might respond to these objections by reaching for
new types of descriptions necessary for an expression to make a certain
reference, or otherwise adjusting what counts as an appropriate
description. Under such alterations the argument from error strikes me
as more resilient. For example, the description theorist might double
down on the "St. Anne" quality of the Cicero example and say: no one in
the immediate community does in fact know enough about Cicero to pick
him out uniquely, all we know is that there is a body of work sitting in
some museum attributed to Cicero, and Cicero is, to present humans,
whoever is responsible for those artifacts. Again, the argument from
error is more resilient to this type of counterargument, as one can
append additional conditions for descriptions that narrow referents down
to an arbitrary degree whilst still leaving the theory open to tailored
counter-examples such as Kripke's or, if correct, mine.

# References 
Kripke, Saul A. (1980). *Naming and Necessity*. Malden, MA:
Blackwell Publishing.
summary:
Saul Kripke, positioned against the description theory of reference (or,
as it is also known, the cluster theory) spends much of his second
lecture in the *Naming and Necessity* series targeting the following two

the cluster a speaker believes to correspond to an expression\], or some
conjointly, are believed by \[the speaker\] to pick out some individual
uniquely," and (3) "if most, or a weighted most, of the \[properties\]
are satisfied by one unique object $\gamma$, then $\gamma$ is the referent of
\[the expression\]" (Saul Kripke, p. 71).^[Page references not otherwise marked
are to Kripke's *Naming and Necessity*.] He does so by means of two arguments,
reserved for

title: Thoughts on Private (Prep) School
content:
Having reached a point in my life where I've met a good number of people with a variety of background whose chief commonality is having earned admittance into a "prestigious" university, I feel inclined (and somewhat well-equipped?) to talk about the benefits/drawbacks/value of a private---specifically, college preparatory---education. I should mention that I best understand "new school", west coast, silicon valley style prep schools as opposed to east coast boarding schools (at any rate, I consider those schools to be distinct enough from most other schools in the country to warrant a separate discussion). There is also the issue of tuition, which I sidestep seeing as it's not a particularly fruitful point (surprise, surprise: these schools can be extremely expensive and, if you come from a middle class background like I did, the expense should be the foremost concern). Modulo the eye-rolling associated with private school culture and rich people and so on, this is all motivated by my considering: *if* I ever have kids, and *if* I have the means to send them to a private school... should I? I think it's an interesting question that maybe doesn't get thought about as much as it should.

The answer obviously depends on who the student in question is and what their needs are. But I think among any student's characteristics, the most relevant is their degree of *drive*. In fact, I think there's a sort of horseshoe theory that one can assign to a student's drive when considering prep schools. In my experience the pattern is as follows: 

1. There are students who are not particularly driven. This may be for a variety of reasons, such as preoccupations with nonacademic concerns, exceptional learning needs, so on. What I've observed is that these students don't fare well in prep schools. These schools (as many know) can attract a lot of competitive students, creating an occasionally tense and unfriendly atmosphere which can be extremely discouraging for students who are struggling to stay on top of work. There is a misconception that prep schools will "straighten out" these students; in my experience the higher-pressure environment doesn't do much more than wreck their self-esteem. This in turn discourages them from catching up with work, and so begins a vicious cycle. There are ways to work around this---hiring private tutors, discussing options with administrators or teachers, working with the student to inspire some more motivation, etc.---and I should admit this is the type of student I've had the least exposure to. Besides, regardless of one's performance, graduating from a prep school does guarantee a base level of competitiveness in a college application, I merely suggest that the the tradeoff this offers over the degree to which the student will struggle drops off with decreasing academic drive. 
2. There are students who are moderately driven. If we are imagining a normal distribution, this is the mean. These students get the most out of prep school, I believe: they are capable of keeping up with the schoolwork, are interested in some of the subjects they encounter in school, and perhaps are involved in (but not exceptionally committed to) extracurriculars. These are the students for whom a prep education will make the most difference, I think, because if they attended public school, they would not have an exceptional application (conversely, their application is boosted by the prep school's reputation), and at the same time they are capable of managing the worst of a prep school. There is also a point to be made about the prep school training the student to have a stronger work ethic, which will help them in college.
3. Then there are students who are "exceptionally" driven. This is the class of student I've spent the most time thinking about regarding the value of prep school. As it stands, I actually think students of this sort can be hindered by prep schools (and I could cite some anecdotal examples. There is one person I can think of who I saw do much better having transferred from a private school to a public school.) The main reason I find this to be the case is because prep schools tend to have a much more "structured" curriculum and whatnot; there is typically a narrower selection of classes, be they in the sciences or the arts, I believe because there are less kids generally and thus these programs have insufficient student interest. As a matter of fact, the lack of students carries consequences for extracurriculars such as clubs---any given activity will garner less interest, or put another way, a student with a specific interest might struggle to find peers who share that interest. A larger school might then provide the student with a larger number of peers who might share interests, leading to the formation of clubs and other things which look good on applications, and perhaps encouraging the student to pursue that interest further. But note this is an issue of school size, not of school type. And, even then, why not just have the student participate in said extracurricular at an outside of school program? This is not a bad idea at all, but I hesitate to immediately embrace it because (1) the workload these prep schools assign can make it difficult to introduce said extracurriculars and (2) the more relaxed workload a public school might assign could provide the student with the time to pursue that extracurricular with sufficiently more conviction to really make a difference in college application. (I suppose there is a third point, namely that the said interest could be academic in nature, i.e., something for which there is no "after school" program; but then here I am implicitly excluding private schools which are targeted toward a specialty, e.g., charter schools.) Good college applicants have good grades; *great* college applicants have good grades *and a small set of extracurricular activities they have pursued with demonstrable conviction*. 

I obviously am course-graining over *a lot*, and I'm not particularly wedded to the model. As with any model, it should be abandoned should the simplifications prove too restrictive. It's all subject to change.

`Edit 10/14/2022:` Grace brought to my attention that it isn't unusual for students to seek out prep schools precisely because they offer strong extracurriculars in addition to strong academics, e.g., sports which are more expensive by nature and thus require more funding. People will seek admittance into these schools with the aim of being recruited (usually, I think, for a niche sport). That's a very good point that the above discussion misses by generalizing prep schools too much. I suppose the takeaway is that it all hinges not only on the student in question but also the school in question.
summary: Having reached a point in my life where I've met a good number of people with a variety of background whose chief commonality is having earned admittance into a "prestigious" university, I feel inclined (and somewhat well-equipped?) to talk about the benefits/drawbacks/value of a private---specifically, college preparatory---education. I should mention that I best understand "new school", west coast, silicon valley style prep schools as opposed to east coast boarding schools (at any rate, I consider those schools to be distinct enough from most other schools in the country to warrant a separate discussion). There is also the issue of tuition, which I sidestep seeing as it's not a particularly fruitful point (surprise, surprise: these schoo
